question.primary_nl: As of January 1st, 2011, which users had a reputation score strictly greater than 1000?

evidence.golden_sql: SELECT displayname FROM users WHERE creationdate < '2011-01-01 00:00:00' AND reputation > 1000;
evidence.reasoning_steps: Filter the users table for registration dates (creationdate) before the reference time and check if their reputation exceeds 1000.

ground_truth.formatted_nl_answer: As of January 1st, 2011, there were 109 users with a reputation score strictly greater than 1000. Some of these users include Shane, csgillespie, Harvey Motulsky, JMS, and JD Long.

question.primary_nl: In the year 2011, which 5 users made the highest number of comments?
nl_variants: Who were the top 5 commenters based on the total number of comments made between Jan 1, 2011 and Dec 31, 2011?
Find the user display names with the most comments in 2011, limiting to the top 5.

evidence.golden_sql: SELECT userdisplayname, COUNT(*) as comment_count FROM comments WHERE creationdate >= '2011-01-01 00:00:00' AND creationdate < '2012-01-01 00:00:00' AND userdisplayname IS NOT NULL GROUP BY userdisplayname ORDER BY comment_count DESC LIMIT 5;
evidence.reasoning_steps: Filter the comments table for creationdate strictly within 2011. Group by userdisplayname, count the records, sort in descending order, and limit to 5.

ground_truth.formatted_nl_answer: In 2011, the top 5 users with the highest number of comments were DBR (32 comments), user5644 (30 comments), user3777 (16 comments), mosaic (9 comments), and andrew cooke (9 comments).

nl_answer_variants.text: The top 5 commenters in 2011 were DBR (32), user5644 (30), user3777 (16), mosaic (9), and andrew cooke (9).

Here are the top 5 chattiest users from 2011! DBR led the pack with 32 comments, followed by user5644 with 30, user3777 with 16, and both mosaic and andrew cooke tied with 9 comments each.

question.primary_nl: Which users earned the 'Promoter' badge before 2013 and also asked at least one question before 2013?

evidence.golden_sql: SELECT DISTINCT u.displayname FROM users u JOIN badges b ON u.id = b.userid WHERE b.name = 'Promoter' AND b.date < '2013-01-01 00:00:00' AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM posts p WHERE p.owneruserid = u.id AND p.posttypeid = 1 AND p.creaiondate < '2013-01-01 00:00:00');
evidence.reasoning_steps: Join users and badges to find 'Promoter' badge earners before 2013. Use an EXISTS clause to check if the same user owns a record in the posts table with posttypeid = 1 and a creation date before 2013.

values: +1 for linking to the full essay rather than one of the all-too-common oversimplifications - it's not long and is still relevant today and well worth reading in full. Hill's criteria for causation are about much more than just pyramid / piling up of evidence though.

Use the trigonometric method for this one and then perform a little algebraic simplification.  About the shortest way to express the solution is $u(z) = 2 \\sin \\left(\\frac{1}{3} \\arcsin(2 z-1)\\right)$.

Hmmm. After much fiddling the solution seemed to be a slight tweak of my y parameter from -0.06 to -0.061 to get convergence for all...However, fewer datasets failed to get convergence using your RHS simplification of the first equation, and all but one converged using the c2=0 simplification. Many thanks.

@fabians: The case I had in mind was sigma^2 = tau^2 = 1 in Theorem 1. It says the horseshoe density is bounded above and below by multiples of log(1 + c/x^2). So maybe the distribution I mentioned above is more of a simplification of the horseshoe density than an approximation.

@user The way to handle complication is to simplify *afterwards.*  When you avoid appropriate analyses in the name of simplification, you lose the opportunity to get superior results.  (Anyway, it is difficult to see how logistic regression would apply to a concept like "quality," which typically is a continuous variable, not a binary one.)

values: And it soon got protected on math.SE, [Does this question even have an answer?](http://math.stackexchange.com/questions/76491/does-this-question-even-have-an-answer) :)

ANOVA viewed through the lens of linear regression theory is, to me, the most appealing and unified method of learning and understanding the topic. A mathematician of most any stripe will feel quite comfortable thinking in terms of linear algebra and, in particular, projections. The $F$-test can then be understood both in terms of ratios of sums of squares and as a monotone transformation of a likelihood-ratio test, which give two complementary viewpoints. For one such text, Seber & Lee (2003), *Linear Regression Analysis*, 2nd edition, Wiley is a choice I can recommend, though it's not free.

@bnaul: Let $F(x) = \\mathbb P(X \\leq x)$ be an *arbitrary* distribution function and let $X_1,\\ldots,X_n$ be an iid sample from $F$. Let $\\newcommand{Xk}{X_{(k)}}\\Xk$ be the $k$th order statistic. Then $$\\mathbb P(\\Xk \\leq x) = \\sum_{m=k}^n \\mathbb P( |\\{i: X_i \\leq x\\}| = m) = \\sum_{m=k}^n {n \\choose m} F(x)^m (1-F(x))^{n-m} \\>.$$ **QED**.

@Hong Ooi:  Thanks!  This code is really useful, and will do the trick, but what I was mainly aiming for was a function which would give a new R model which I could then put into predict() later on in the workflow.  However, I think your function is pretty elegant, and like the way you can set it to give probabilities.

evidence.golden_sql: SELECT DISTINCT u.displayname FROM users u JOIN posts p ON u.id = p.owneruserid WHERE p.posttypeid = 2 AND p.creaiondate >= '2011-01-01 00:00:00' AND p.creaiondate < '2012-01-01 00:00:00' AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM comments c WHERE c.postid = p.id AND c.score > 10 AND c.creationdate < '2012-01-01 00:00:00');
evidence.reasoning_steps: The query identifies answer authors in 2011. It uses an EXISTS subquery to check the comments table for a specific quality threshold (score > 10) within the same year.
